coredata.dc:title: Chemical redox modulated fluorescence of nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots for probing the activity of alkaline phosphatase
coredata.dc:creator: Liu, JingJing
Tang, Duosi
Chen, Zhitao
Yan, Xiaomei
Zhong, Zhou
Kang, Longtian
Yao, Jiannian
coredata.dc:description: Alkaline phosphatase (ALP) as an essential enzyme plays an important role in clinical diagnoses and biomedical researches. Hence, the development of convenient and sensitivity assay for monitoring ALP is extremely important. In this work, on the basis of chemical redox strategy to modulate the fluorescence of nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots (NGQDs), a novel label-free fluorescent sensing system for the detection of alkaline phosphatase (ALP) activity has been developed. The fluorescence of NGQDs is firstly quenched by ultrathin cobalt oxyhydroxide (CoOOH) nanosheets, and then restored by ascorbic acid (AA), which can reduce CoOOH to Co2+, thus the ALP can be monitored based on the enzymatic hydrolysis of L-ascorbic acid-2-phosphate (AAP) by ALP to generate AA. Quantitative evaluation of ALP activity in a range from 0.1 to 5U/L with the detection limit of 0.07U/L can be realized in this sensing system. Endowed with high sensitivity and selectivity, the proposed assay is capable of detecting ALP in biological system with satisfactory results. Meanwhile, this sensing system can be easily extended to the detection of various AA-involved analytes.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Chemical redox
Nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots
Ultrathin cobalt oxyhydroxide nanosheets
Alkaline phosphatase
Fluorescence

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis of nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots (NGQDs)
Analysis of ALP activity in aqueous solution
Procedure for determining ALP level in human serum
Monitoring of ALP activity using pNPP-based assay protocol
Characterizations and optical properties of NGQDs and CoOOH
Kinetic and time-resolved fluorescence decay assays
The fluorescence recovery of NGQDs by AA

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) TEM and HRTEM (inset) images of the as prepared NGQDs. (b) Diameter distribution of NGQDs. (c) AFM image of NGQDs deposited on the mica substrate. (d) Height distribution of NGQDs.
(a) XRD pattern of the CoOOH nanosheets. (b) TEM image of CoOOH. (c) AFM image of CoOOH nanosheets deposited on the mica substrate. (d) The corresponding height profile of CoOOH nanosheets.

(a) TEM image of CoOOH-NGQDs probe. (b) UV–vis absorption spectrum of ultrathin CoOOH nanosheets and the fluorescence emission spectrum of the NGQDs. (c) Fluorescence of NGQDs with the addition of different concentrations of CoOOH nanosheets. (d) Fluorescence emission spectra of NGQDs (curve a), CoOOH-NGQDs (curve b), and CoOOH-NGQDs in the present of AA (curve c). (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.) (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

(a) Fluorescence spectra of CoOOH-NGQDs in the presence of different concentrations of ALP. (b) The relationship between [(F−F0)/F0] and the concentration of ALP. F0 and F are fluorescence intensities without and with ALP, respectively. The inset shows a linearity relationship in the concentration range from 0.1 to 5.0U/L. The data shown in the figures represent the average of three independent experiments (n =3). (c) Specificity of the NGQDs-CoOOH nanoprobe against ALP, PPase, PP1, Thrombin, GOx, Trypsin, Gal and Lys.
Comparison of the assay results toward ALP in serum specimens using the developed assay and the pNPP-based standard method, respectively. Error bar represents the standard deviation (n =3).
Turn-on fluorescence sensing of ALP using ultrathin CoOOH modified NGQDs.

head.ce:title: Chemical redox modulated fluorescence of nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots for probing the activity of alkaline phosphatase

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of Design and Assembly of Functional Nanostructures, Chinese Academy of Sciences, Fuzhou 350002, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Design and Assembly of Functional Nanostructures, Chinese Academy of Sciences

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Fujian Provincial Key Laboratory of Nanomaterials, Fujian Institute of Research on the Structure of Matter, Chinese Academy of Sciences, Fuzhou

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Chemistry, Fuzhou University, Fuzhou, Fujian 350002, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Chemistry, Fuzhou University, Fuzhou

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Fuqing Entry-Exit Inspection and Quarantine Bureau, Fuqing, Fujian 350002, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Fuqing Entry-Exit Inspection and Quarantine Bureau, Fuqing

ce:affiliation.ce:label: e
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Beijing National Laboratory for Molecular Sciences (BNLMS), Institute of Chemistry, Chinese Academy of Sciences, Beijing 100190, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Beijing National Laboratory for Molecular Sciences (BNLMS), Institute of Chemistry, Chinese Academy of Sciences

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author at: Key Laboratory of Design and Assembly of Functional Nanostructures, Chinese Academy of Sciences, Fuzhou 350002, PR China.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Alkaline phosphatase (ALP) as an essential enzyme plays an important role in clinical diagnoses and biomedical researches. Hence, the development of convenient and sensitivity assay for monitoring ALP is extremely important. In this work, on the basis of chemical redox strategy to modulate the fluorescence of nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots (NGQDs), a novel label-free fluorescent sensing system for the detection of alkaline phosphatase (ALP) activity has been developed. The fluorescence of NGQDs is firstly quenched by ultrathin cobalt oxyhydroxide (CoOOH) nanosheets, and then restored by ascorbic acid (AA), which can reduce CoOOH to Co2+, thus the ALP can be monitored based on the enzymatic hydrolysis of L-ascorbic acid-2-phosphate (AAP) by ALP to generate AA. Quantitative evaluation of ALP activity in a range from 0.1 to 5U/L with the detection limit of 0.07U/L can be realized in this sensing system. Endowed with high sensitivity and selectivity, the proposed assay is capable of detecting ALP in biological system with satisfactory results. Meanwhile, this sensing system can be easily extended to the detection of various AA-involved analytes.

ce:section.ce:para: Alkaline phosphatase (ALP), which can catalyses the hydrolysis of phosphoryl esters in alkaline media, is an essential enzyme and widely distributes in a variety of tissues (Deng et al., 2015). Since an abnormal level of serum ALP is often correlated with bone disease, liver dysfunction, heart failure, ovarian and breast cancer, ALP can be used as a biomarker of disease (Liang et al., 2013). In addition, ALP is also used as labelling enzymes in immunoassays and related affinity sensing methods for monitoring proteins, nucleic acids and other analytes (Hu et al., 2016; Ma et al., 2014). Thus, developing convenient and reliable assay for monitoring ALP activity is significant in clinical diagnoses and biomedical researches.

In this regard, considerable efforts have been made to the development of rapid and convenient determination systems for assay the activity of ALP. These involve the electrochemical (Murata et al., 2009), chemiluminescence (Yu et al., 2014), colorimetric determination (Zhao et al., 2007; Wei et al., 2008; Li et al., 2013), chromatographic (Hasegawa et al., 2006) and surface enhanced resonance Raman scattering (Ingram et al., 2009). Due to its simplicity, convenience and high sensitivity, the fluorescence method shows great advantages in the detection of ALP activity (Ma et al., 2011). Several different indicators, such as CdS quantum dots (Liu et al., 2014), tetraphenylethylene (Liang et al., 2013), DNA (Zhang et al., 2013), and Au nanoclusters (Hu et al., 2016) have been designed for the determination of ALP. However, their costly and complex synthesis, poor photostability, toxic constituents and poor biocompatibility are serious obstacles for further monitoring ALP level changes in physiological process.

As a new class of promising fluorescent materials, graphene quantum dots (GQDs), which combine the excellent properties of graphene and quantum dots, have received much attention in bioimaging (Q. Liu et al., 2013; Zheng et al., 2013; Tan et al., 2015), sensors (Zhou et al., 2013; J.J. Liu et al., 2013; Qian et al., 2014; Lin et al., 2014; Benítez-Martínez and Valcárcel, 2015; Liu et al., 2015; Zheng et al., 2015) photodynamic therapy (Ge et al., 2014) and optoelectronic device (Zhang et al., 2012; Wang et al., 2015; X. Li et al., 2015). Doping GQDs with heteroatoms (such as nitrogen, boron, sulfur atoms) can effectively tune the electron density, tailor the photonic properties, and improve the quantum yield (QY) of GQDs (Qu et al., 2014; Ju and Chen, 2014; S. Li et al., 2014; Zhang et al., 2015). Especially, the nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots (NGQDs) with high QY were demonstrated and showed attractive applications (Li et al., 2012; Qu et al., 2013; Wu et al., 2014).

Recently, a new strategy was introduced for the assay of reactive species based on the specific redox reaction between layered transition-metal oxide nanomaterials (LTMN, for instance, MnO2 and CoOOH) and reactive species. LTMN have been verified as promising fluorescence quenchers due to their excellent light absorption capability and fast electron transfer rate (Deng et al., 2011; N. Li et al., 2014; Cai et al., 2015). For example, endow with the specific reaction of CoOOH and AA, Tang's group developed a nanoprobe for detection and imaging of AA in living cells and in vivo using CoOOH-modified persistent luminescence nanoparticles (N. Li et al., 2014). Li's group described a fluorescence “switch-on” assay for glutathione detection by using carbon dtos-MnO2 nanocomposites (Cai et al., 2015). However, the use of relatively thick CoOOH or MnO2 nanosheets may lead to the poor stability of nanoprobe and the decrease of sensitivity.

Herein, we propose a sensitive and selective fluorescent turn-on nanoprobe for the assay of ALP activity by employing ultrathin CoOOH nanosheets and NGQDs as energy acceptor and donor, respectively. The principle of this strategy was demonstrated in Scheme 1. Because the emission spectrum of the NGQDs overlaps with the absorption spectrum of ultrathin CoOOH nanosheets, the fluorescence of NGQDs can be efficiently quenched by CoOOH via fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET). The ALP can hydrolyze L-ascorbic acid-2-phosphate (AAP) to produce AA, which could reduce CoOOH to Co2+ ions due to the specific redox reaction between CoOOH and AA, leading to destruction of the NGQDs-CoOOH complex and then release of NGQDs.

Citric acid (CA) and ethylenediamine (EA) were purchased from Aladdin Chemistry Co., Ltd. Ascorbic acid (AA), glutathione (GSH), cysteine (Cys), dialysis bag (retained molecular weight: 1000Da), tris (hydroxymethyl)-aminomethane (Tris), diethanolamine (DEA) were purchased from Shanghai Sangon Biotechnology Co. Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Dopamine, urea, alkaline phosphate (ALP), L-ascorbic acid-2-phosphate (AAP), 4-nitrophenyl phosphate disodium salt hexahydrate (pNPP), serine-threonine protein phosphatase 1 (PP1), pyrophosphatase (PPase), thrombin, lysozyme, trypsin, glucose oxidase (GOx) and galactosidase (Gal) were obtained from Sigma-Aldrich Chemical Co. (USA). Concentrated hydrochloric acid (HCl), N,N-dimethyl formamide (DMF), cobalt chloride hexahydrate (CoCl2·6H2O), sodium hypochlorite (NaClO), sodium hydroxide (NaOH), copper sulfate pentahydrate (CuSO4·5H2O), nickel chloride hexahydrate (NiCl2·6H2O), zinc nitrate hexahydrate (Zn(NO3)2·6H2O), iron (Ⅲ) chloride hexahydrate (FeCl3·6H2O), iron (Ⅱ) chloride tetrahydrate (FeCl2·4H2O), sodium chloride (NaCl), potassium chloride (KCl), calcium chloride (CaCl2), magnesium chloride (MgCl2), sodium phosphate monobasic dihydrate (NaH2PO4·2H2O) and sodium phosphate dibasic dodecahydrate (Na2HPO4·12H2O) were purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent co., Ltd. (China). Human serum was kindly provided by Fuqing Entry-Exit Inspection and Quarantine Bureau (China). All reagents were of analytical grade, and were used without further purification. Milli-Q ultrapure water with a resistivity of 18.2MΩcm was used in all experiments.

Transmission electron microscope (TEM) and high resolution transmission electron microscopic (HRTEM) measurements were performed using a JEOL-2010 (Jeol, Japan) at an acceleration voltage of 200kV. Atomic force microscopic (AFM) images were taken out using a Dimension ICON scanning probe microscope (Bruker, Germany). The X-ray photoelectron spectra (XPS) were recorded on an Escalab 250Xi spectrometer (Thermo Scientific, USA). The scanning electron microscopy (SEM) experiments were carried out on a JSM 6700 (Jeol, Japan) at 10.0kV. The crystal structure was identified by powder X-ray diffraction (XRD) analysis (Rigaku, Japan) using Miniflex ⅡX-ray diffractometer with Cu Kα radiation in the 2θ range 10–70°. UV–vis absorption spectra were measured using a UV-2600 spectrophotometer (Shimadzu, Japan) at room temperature. Raman spectra were obtained on a LabRAM HR Raman microspectrometer (HORIBA Jobin-Yvon, France). Fluorescence spectra were collected by using a Hitachi F-4600 fluorometer (Hitachi Co. Ltd., Japan). The time-resolved fluorescence decay was performed on an Edinburgh FLS920 spectrofluorometer (Edinburgh, UK) with a 370nm LED as the excitation source. The pH measurements were performed with a PHS-2F digital acidity meter (Leici, Shanghai).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots (NGQDs)
ce:section.ce:para: 0.387g CA and 0.534g EA was dissolved into 70mL water, and ultrasonic for 10min to form a homogeneous solution. Then, the solution was transferred to a 100mL Teflon lined autoclave and heated at 200°C for 12h. After being cooled to room temperature, the resulted solution was evaporated to dryness using reduced pressure rotary evaporation at 50°C. The residue was redissolved in 70mL DMF. After ultrasonic for 10min, the solution was transferred to a 100mL Teflon lined autoclave and heated at 250°C for 12h. After being cooled to room temperature, the larger products were filtered out using 0.22µm microporous membrane and a brown filter solution was separated. The colloidal solution was further dialyzed in a 1000Da dialysis bag against water for 2 days to remove DMF and excess inorganic salt, an orange solution of NGQDs was obtained.

In a typical reaction, 300μL of NaOH (1M) was added to 2mL of CoCl2 (5mM) solution and then sonicated for 1min. Subsequently, 50μL of NaClO (0.9M) was added to the mixture and sonicated for 10min. And then the product was collected by centrifugation at 10,000rpm for 15min, washed with water several times and finally sonicated for more than 12h. To remove possible nanoparticles after ultrasonic treatment, the dispersions were centrifuged at 2000rpm for 10min and the brown suspension was collected.

On the basis of the above procedure, the sensing solution was prepared by mixing ultrathin CoOOH nanosheets and NGQD for 2min at room temperature. The final concentration of CoOOH and NGQDs was 25μgmL−1 and 3.5μgmL−1, respectively. Then, the fluorescence intensity of the mixtures was measured at 425nm with excitation at 337nm.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analysis of ALP activity in aqueous solution
ce:section.ce:para: In a typical assay, 50μL reaction solutions (Tris–HCl, 10mM, pH 9.0) containing different concentration of ALP and 1mM AAP were mixed in a 0.5mL micro-centrifuge tube. Then, these samples were incubated at 37°C for 30min. After incubation, 200μL of the prepared NGQDs-CoOOH nanoprobe was added, reaching a total volume of 250μL for each sample. The fluorescence emission was measured after equilibrium for 10min at room temperature.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Procedure for determining ALP level in human serum
ce:section.ce:para: Aliquots of 50-fold diluted human serum with Tris-HCl (pH 9.0) were mixed with 1.0mM AAP. Samples were then incubated at 37°C for 30min. After incubation, 200μL of the prepared NGQDs-CoOOH nanoprobe was added, reaching a total volume of 250μL for each sample. The mixtures were incubated at room temperature for another 10min. Subsequently, the fluorescence intensity of the mixtures was measured at 425nm with excitation at 337nm.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Monitoring of ALP activity using pNPP-based assay protocol
ce:section.ce:para: Aliquots of 20-fold diluted human serum with DEA (pH 10.0) were mixed with 5.0mM pNPP. The final volume was 250μL. Then, the resulting mixture was incubated 60min at 37°C. After that, UV–vis absorption of the mixture was recorded.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterizations and optical properties of NGQDs and CoOOH
ce:section.ce:para: To realize the above design, the NGQDs were prepared using hydrothermal method combined with a solvothermal method. TEM images ( Fig. 1a and b) show the uniformly dispersed NGQDs with an average of 3nm. The high resolution TEM image (Fig. 1a, inset) clearly shows the lattice fringes of an NGQD with a d spacing of 0.24 fit with the (1120) facet of graphene (M. Zheng et al., 2013; X.T. Zheng et al., 2013). AFM images shown in Fig. 1c and Fig. 1d reveal that the average thickness of the obtained NGQDs is about 1.2nm. The average ζ-potential of the NGQDs is measured to be about ­13.3mV. Moreover, XPS, Raman, UV–vis absorption and fluorescence spectra characterizations were carried out to explore the properties of the NGQDs (Fig. S1 and Fig. S2, details are in Supplementary materials). All the results indicate that the NGQDs should be excellent energy donor candidates in FRET-based sensing platform.

As energy acceptor, ultrathin CoOOH nanosheets were also synthesized by the previously reported method with a slight modification (L. Li et al., 2015). The XRD patterns ( Fig. 2a) indicate that the prepared CoOOH nanosheets exist in hexagonal rhomb-centered phase (JCPDS 07-0169) without any impurities. SEM (Fig. S3) and TEM images (Fig. 2b) clearly reveal the typical hexagon nanosheets morphology of CoOOH. Analysis of AFM images shows that the obtained CoOOH nanosheets present an obvious single-layer sheet structure, with a thickness of about 1.5nm (Fig. 2c and d). XPS and UV–vis spectroscopy measurements were taken and the results are shown in Fig. S4 and Fig. S5. The zeta potential of the CoOOH nanosheets was 8.03mV, showing the positive charge of the nanosheets. Therefore, all above results clearly demonstrate that ultrathin CoOOH nanosheets were successfully fabricated.

Due to the electrostatic and hydrogen-bond interactions between NGQDs and ultrathin CoOOH nanosheets, the CoOOH-NGQDs nanoprobe was fabricated by simple incubation of the NGQDs with the ultrathin CoOOH nanosheets. Fig. S6 shows the UV–vis absorption spectra of CoOOH nanosheets and CoOOH-NGQDs composite. It can be seen that the CoOOH-NGQDs composite exhibits an absorption band centered at 415nm, which has a red-shift of 7nm compared to that of CoOOH nanosheets. In addition, TEM image ( Fig. 3a) clearly shows that the NGQDs are well interspersed on the surface of ultrathin COOOH nanosheets, which confirms the successful obtainment of CoOOH-NGQDs nanoprobe. Note that the absorption of ultrathin CoOOH nanosheets overlaps with the emissions of the NGQDs, thus enabling the generation of FRET (Fig. 3b). Subsequently, the fluorescence quenching of NGQDs upon modification of different concentration of CoOOH nanosheets were investigated (Fig. 3c). As expected, the fluorescence of NGQDs decreased gradually with increased CoOOH nanosheets concentration, and trend to a minimum value at 30μgmL−1. However, when AA was added into CoOOH-NGQDs sensing system, CoOOH nanosheets were reduced to Co2+ and the quenched fluorescence was recovered (Fig. 3d). As well known, the metal ions are common effective quenchers to NGQDs, therefore, the effect of Co2+ on the fluorescence of NGQDs was also investigated. Fig. S7 shows that Co2+ from 40 to 1000μM has not distinct interference with the probe.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Kinetic and time-resolved fluorescence decay assays
ce:section.ce:para: The kinetic behaviour of the CoOOH-NGQDs probe with AA was studied by monitoring the fluorescence intensity as a function of time. As shown in Fig. S8, the quenching of NGQDs by CoOOH nanosheets is a fairly rapid process, it reached equilibrium in 2min. In contrast, the reduction and decomposition of CoOOH nanosheets are relatively slow and need almost 10min to reach the equilibrium. To better understand the quenching process, the time-resolved fluorescence decay of NGQDs in the absence and presence of CoOOH nanosheets were studied. Decay profiles are shown in Fig. S9 and Table S1. Both samples show three exponential decay curves with short-lived, moderate-lived and long-lived component. Generally, the emission that originates form defect states shows a longer recombination lifetime than that from intrinsic states (F. Liu et al., 2013; Zhu et al., 2012; Huang et al., 2014). The nitrogen-doping introduces a new kind of surface states (N-states) (Dong et al., 2013). So the three components probably respectively correspond to recombination from the intrinsic states, N-states and defect states. Moreover, the defect states play the leading role in NGQDs and the calculated average lifetime value is around of 7.26ns. However, in the present of ultrathin CoOOH nanosheets, the defect states emission were suppressed, making the intrinsic states play the leading role in CoOOH-NGQDs composite and a decrease of lifetime of 5.34ns was observed. The result reveals that the quenching mechanism of NGQDs by ultrathin CoOOH nanosheets was a dynamic quenching mechanism. The quenching mechanism was further supported by the increased lifetime after adding AA. In the presence of AA, AA can reduce the CoOOH nanosheets in CoOOH-NGQDs, lead to decompose of the hybrid structure and the defect states emission were recovered. All above results indicated that CoOOH nanosheets can quench the fluorescence of NGQDs by the dynamic quenching process and then are decomposed to Co2+ by the AA through the specific redox reaction.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The fluorescence recovery of NGQDs by AA
ce:section.ce:para: The sensitivity of the CoOOH-NGQDs for fluorescent probing of AA was evaluated. As indicated in Fig. S10a, the fluorescence intensity of NGQDs after quenching by CoOOH nanosheets is sensitive to AA, and increase with the increasing concentration of AA from 0.05μM to 100μM. Fig. S10b shows the plot of fluorescence enhancement factors [(F−F0)/F0] against the concentration of AA. A linear correlation (R2=0.9979) exists between the value of fluorescence enhancement factors and the concentration of AA over the range 0.05–2μM (Fig. S10b, inset). And the detection limit as low as 35nM could be achieved based on the definition of three times the deviation of the blank signal (3σ). Table S2 lists the comparison of proposed sensing system and other approaches for the detection of AA. Note that, the limit of detection of CoOOH-NGQDs is better than many other reported methods.

The selectivity of AA detection was also evaluated. As shown in Fig. S11a, only the addition of AA resulted in a prominent fluorescence change, whereas very weak variations of the fluorescence of NGQDs were observed upon the addition of 100-fold excess of all the metal ions. And the sensing system exhibits a slight fluorescence response to other common reductive species such as urine acid, dopamine, glutathione (GSH) and cysteine (Cys). These results revealed that the proposed CoOOH-NGQDs could be used as a sensitive and selective platform for AA detection.

Moreover, endow with the high sensitivity and selectivity, the proposed assay is capable of detecting AA in diluted human plasma. As shown in Fig. S11b, the present probe provides a linear response to AA spiked into human plasma. Taking the 50-fold dilution into calculation, the concentration of AA in human plasma is 76μM. The estimated value agrees with the normal level of AA between 0.6 and 1.5mgmL−1 in human plasma (Chen and Yan, 2009)..

ALP, which had been proved has the best activity at an alkaline environment and appropriate incubation temperature around 37°C, can hydrolyze L-ascorbic acid-2-phosphate (AAP) to produce AA (Zhang et al., 2016). To establish the sensitive detection of ALP, a series of tests was performed to optimize the sensing parameters, such as, reaction time, pH of the buffer solution, and AAP concentration. We firstly studied the effect of reaction time on the fluorescence intensity of CoOOH-NGQDs. As shown in Fig. S12a, the relative fluorescence intensity significantly increased with time while the value reached a platform after 30min. Therefore, 30min was chosen as the enzymatic reaction time in this work. As for the pH value, the effect of pH within a range from 8 to 10 was performed. The result in Fig. S12b indicates that the optimum pH of Tris buffer used in this study is 9.0. Then, the effect of AAP concentration on the fluorescence intensity was also studied. The result in Fig. S12c shows that, the recovered fluorescence increased with the AAP concentration and reached the maximum at the final concentration of 1.0mM.

Under the optimum conditions, the fluorescence intensity of the sensing system incubated with ALP at different concentration is monitored. The quenched fluorescence of NGQDs by CoOOH increased steadily with the increasing ALP concentration from 0.1 to 200U/L ( Fig. 4a). Furthermore, a good linear relationship between the fluorescence enhancement factors [(F−F0)/F0] against and the ALP concentration in the range from 0.1 to 5U/L was plotted in the inset of Fig. 4b. And the limit detection was evaluated to be 0.07U/L, which is much lower or comparable with that obtained by previous reports (Table S3).

To demonstrate the selectivity of the CoOOH-NGQDs probe for ALP, control experiments were conducted using pyrophosphatase (PPase), serine-threonine protein phosphatase 1 (PP1), thrombin, glucose oxidase (GOx), trypsin, galactosidase (Gal) and lysozyme (Lys). As shown in Fig. 4c, the addition of other protein into the sensing system did not lead to a remarkable change in the fluorescence spectra. In contrast, upon the addition of ALP, the fluorescence of the NGQDs-CoOOH significantly changed. These results substantially suggest that the sensing system had a high selectivtity toward ALP.

Since the normal range of ALP in adults is reported to be 46–190U/L (Li et al., 2013), the as-established CoOOH-NGQDs assay is sensitive enough for paratical detection of ALP level in human body. In this case, ten different serum specimens were diluted and monitored by using the designed assay protocol. Further, the obtained data on the ALP activity in these serum samples were compared with those form the traditional pNPP-based standard assay method. Fig. 5 shows the comparative results with these samples between two methods. As analyzed from these experimental data, a highly positive correlation between the two methods was established, with a slope of 1.01±0.01, an intercept of −1.76±1.67, and a correlation coefficient (R2) of 0.9982. The results demonstrating that the data obtained from the proposed method were in good agreement with those from the standard ALP assay. Thus the NGQDs-CoOOH assay was reliable for the quantitative detection of ALP in real sample.

In summary, we have successfully developed a simple fluorescent assay for highly selective and sensitive monitoring of ALP activity based on the chemical redox-regulated fluorescence of NGQDs. NGQDs was employed as the energy donor, and ultrathin CoOOH nanosheets were used as the energy acceptor. The CoOOH-induced quenching effect can be reversed in the presence of AA, which is the hydrolysis product of AAP by ALP. Therefore, the proposed assay demonstrates a linear range from 0.1 to 5U/L with the detection limit of 0.07U/L toward ALP detection. Moreover, this assay was applied to determine the ALP level in human serum samples and the results were matched well with the pNPP-based standard method. Furthermore, it can be extended for probing various AA-involved analytes by simply changing the substrate. Given its simplicity, cost-effectiveness and sensitivity, this assay provides a promising potential in clinical applications.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors gratefully acknowledge the financial from the National Natural Science Foundation of China (21473204), the Special Project of National Major Scientific Equipment Development of China (2012YQ120060), the Natural Science Foundation of Fujian Province (2015J01070), and the Science and Technology Planning Project of Fujian Province (2014H2008).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.bios.2017.03.017.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
Supplementary material
.
